prevalence.value: ~10-20 per 1,000 live births (higher in some settings)
prevalence.source: National Neonatology Forum of India (NNF) / WHO newborn health estimates
prevalence.trend: decreasing in facilities with improved infection prevention; high burden persists in low-resource settings

burden.dalyPerCapita: high per-case; major contributor to neonatal mortality
burden.mortalityRate: Case-fatality commonly ~10-30% depending on facility and resistance patterns
burden.source: WHO / IHME GBD neonatal infection estimates

riskFactors: home births or delayed facility access
overcrowding and limited infection prevention resources
vulnerablePopulations: neonates in high-volume public maternity hospitals
rural and low-income families with delayed referral
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical syndrome with lab support (blood culture, CBC, CRP/procalcitonin) where available; sepsis bundles per national neonatal protocols

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no screening; early recognition in SNCU/NICU and postnatal follow-up programmes
publicHealthSystem.estimatedPatients: hundreds of thousands/year (high national birth cohort)
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Variable; improved in facilities with strong SNCU/NICU capacity but affected by resistance and delayed presentation

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: SNCU/NICU protocols with clinical triggers; culture and sensitivity testing varies by district
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Empiric antibiotics per national guidance (ampicillin + gentamicin for early-onset; broader regimens for late-onset guided by local resistance); supportive care in SNCU/NICU
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: High caseloads, antimicrobial resistance, uneven NICU capacity, and delayed referral from peripheral facilities
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Expanding SNCU network and national focus on newborn survival with standardized facility-based care protocols
